metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the single `grep -c` call in AC2 with per-file assertions that verify each workflow independently, e.g. `grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml | grep -qv '^0$' && grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/test.yml | grep -qv '^0$'`.
evidence: AC2 text: 'grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml is >=1 for EACH'

With multiple-file grep -c, exit code is 0 if any file matches; per-file counts are printed but not asserted programmatically — verify-command defect: missing cardinality assertion per file (taxonomy item #5/scope-mismatch)
findings.finding: AC2's `grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' file1 file2` command returns exit 0 if the pattern appears in ANY of the listed files, not necessarily BOTH — so the gate could be present in only one workflow and the AC would still appear to pass, silently missing the other file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`grep -c 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml`)
scenarios: An executor updates only one workflow file; AC2 grep-c returns exit 0 (one file matches), criterion appears satisfied, but the other workflow still reads the allowlist.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace AC2's grep command with two independent checks:
```
grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml \
  && grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' .github/workflows/test.yml
```
This asserts the pattern is present in EACH file and exits non-zero if either is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify command to AC2 that confirms `module-size-ceilings.txt` reads are also absent from both workflow files (e.g., `! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/test.yml`).
evidence: What item 2e: 'The step no longer reads `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` or `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (in EITHER workflow)'
AC2 verify command: '`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml`' — only checks allowlist, not ceilings
.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml:165: `ceilings=.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (current, must be removed)
.github/workflows/test.yml:209: `ceilings=.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (current, must be removed)
findings.finding: The plan's What item 2e states 'The step no longer reads `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` or `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (in EITHER workflow)', but AC2's verification command only checks for removal of `module-size-allowlist` reads (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist'…`) from both workflow files — there is no corresponding check that `module-size-ceilings.txt` reads are also removed. This means the ceilings-file read (currently `ceilings=.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` in both workflows) could survive in the rewritten gate without any AC catching it.
findings.location: What item 2e; Acceptance Criteria bullet 2
scenarios: Implementer rewrites the gate but accidentally leaves a `ceilings=.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` line in one workflow (perhaps in a comment block or dead code). AC2 passes because it only greps for `module-size-allowlist`. The ceilings read is technically unreachable but the file reference survives, causing confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC2 bullet's verify block to: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify command to AC2 (or a new AC) that greps for absence of 'module-size-ceilings' in both workflow files, matching the deliverable stated in What 2e.
evidence: What 2e: 'The step no longer reads .github/module-size-allowlist.txt or .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (in EITHER workflow).'

AC2 verify: '! grep -q \'module-size-allowlist\' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q \'module-size-allowlist\' .github/workflows/test.yml' — ceilings file never appears in any AC verify command.
findings.finding: What item 2e states the gate 'no longer reads .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (in EITHER workflow)', but AC2's verify command only greps for 'module-size-allowlist' removal — there is no acceptance criterion that verifies the ceilings-file read is also removed from both workflows.
findings.location: What item 2e; AC2 (second bullet)
scenarios: An implementer removes the allowlist read but accidentally leaves a ceilings read; all ACs pass but the described deliverable is not met.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's verify line: append `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the 'not a positive int' validation branch (e.g. set `.github/module-size-limit.txt` to `0` or `abc`, run the gate step, and assert exit 1 with a descriptive error message).
evidence: Step 2a: 'Read LIMIT=$(cat .github/module-size-limit.txt) (fail if the file is missing or not a positive int)'
AC4 only tests the missing-file case ('remove .github/module-size-limit.txt, run it → exit 1 with a must not be removed message')
ABSENCE: no AC tests the case where the file exists but contains e.g. '0', '-1', or 'abc'
findings.finding: Step 2a specifies 'fail if the file is missing OR not a positive int' but no acceptance criterion maps to the 'not a positive int' branch — its error message and exit behavior are unverified.
findings.location: What — Step 2a; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers the non-positive-int validation path)
scenarios: A contributor sets the limit file to '0' or 'abc'; the gate silently passes (or crashes non-deterministically) because no AC verified this branch's guard.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to ACs:
```
- [ ] The gate FAILS when the limit file contains a non-positive integer (verify: echo '0' > .github/module-size-limit.txt && <run gate> → exit 1 with an 'invalid limit' message; echo 'abc' > .github/module-size-limit.txt → same)
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify step (analogous to AC4/AC5) that runs the gate shell snippet with a malformed limit file (e.g., containing 'abc' or '0') and asserts it exits 1 with an appropriate error message.
evidence: What 2a: 'fail if the file is missing or not a positive int'
AC4 verify tests only the missing-file case: 'remove .github/module-size-limit.txt, run it → exit 1 with a must not be removed message'
AC6 verify: 'pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py -q' — this tests the committed file contents, not the shell gate's parse-failure branch.
findings.finding: What item 2a specifies the gate must 'fail if the file is missing OR not a positive int', but no AC tests the shell gate's runtime behavior when the limit file contains a non-positive-integer value (e.g., '0', '-1', 'foo'). AC6 (pytest) verifies the file content at commit time is a positive int, but does not test the gate script's error-handling path for a malformed file.
findings.location: What item 2a; AC4 (fourth bullet); AC6 (sixth bullet)
scenarios: An implementer implements the missing-file guard but forgets to add the integer-validation branch; all ACs pass but the gate silently treats '0' or 'abc' as a valid limit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'The gate fails when the limit file is non-positive-integer (verify: replace .github/module-size-limit.txt contents with "0" / "abc", run gate step → exit 1 with a parse-error message).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC4's pattern) that runs the gate step against a crafted tree containing a synthetic >800-line `.py` file and asserts the step exits non-zero with a descriptive error message.
evidence: AC4: 'extract the gate step, remove .github/module-size-limit.txt, run it → exit 1' (tests missing-file guard only)
AC5: 'BASE=800 LIMIT=900 → exit 1' (tests limit-lock only)
AC2: grep-based structural check that the limit file is referenced
AC6: pytest verifies actual repo files — but all are already under 800 per the epic; the FAIL branch of the cap check is never exercised
ABSENCE: no AC says 'create a temp file with 801 lines, run the gate step → exit 1 with an oversized-file message'
findings.finding: The plan's ACs include simulated tests for the missing-file guard (AC4) and limit-lock (AC5) by running crafted shell snippets, but NO AC simulates the core absolute-cap enforcement path — i.e., running the gate against a crafted file tree containing a `.py` file with >800 lines to assert exit 1. All ACs that touch the cap either do a structural grep (AC2: limit file referenced) or verify actual repo files are already under 800 (AC6/pytest), so a bug in the `wc -l` comparison logic would go undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC4, AC5, AC6 (no AC covers 'oversized file → gate exits 1')
scenarios: A typo in the `wc -l` comparison (e.g., `>=` vs `>`, off-by-one, wrong glob) would not be caught — all ACs would still pass while the gate silently mis-fires on production.
A future refactor of the gate shell replaces the comparison operator; no AC fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Oversized-file cap test: create a temp dir with src/rebar/big.py containing 801 lines, set LIMIT=800, run the gate step → assert exit 1 and output names the offending file.' Mirror the AC4 approach (extract step, craft inputs, assert exit code).

findings.finding: What item 3 says to 'drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions' from `test_module_size_contract.py`, but AC bullet 4 only verifies that the test *passes* (`pytest … -q`). Since the legacy files are kept (non-empty content is irrelevant but they exist), old sync assertions might still pass if the files are present — a passing run does not confirm the old assertions were removed.
findings.location: What item 3 / AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC bullet 4's verify: `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist\|module-size-ceilings' tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the shell gate's absolute-cap enforcement end-to-end (running the actual shell step against a tree that contains an over-cap file). The Notes section acknowledges 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' but the ACs only grep for strings in the workflow files or run Python tests — neither actually executes the shell gate against a crafted tree.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all AC bullets; Notes section ('E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Simulate cap enforcement: create a temp file of 801 lines; run the gate snippet `LIMIT=800; wc -l <file> | awk ...`; assert exit 1. Create a file of 800 lines; assert exit 0.' This can be a small shell script or an additional pytest parametrized case in the contract test.

findings.finding: The absolute-cap failure path of the new gate script (a file with wc -l > $LIMIT causing an exit 1) has no behavioral acceptance criterion that actually runs the gate against a crafted over-cap tree. The Notes state the oracle is 'running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but no AC exercises this path; AC2 only greps for the limit-file reference structurally, and AC4 tests the contract on the real codebase (all files already under cap).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / Notes (E5 sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'The gate rejects an over-cap file: create a temp file with 801 lines under src/rebar/, run the gate step (or the relevant shell snippet), assert exit 1; a 800-line file passes (exit 0). Verify: `python -c "open('/tmp/src/rebar/big.py','w').write('x\n'*801)"` then run the cap-check snippet.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update parent epic 716f to explicitly describe the 2a/2b split (replacing 'one child story' with the two-phase plan) and adjust AC2 to reflect that file deletion lands in Phase 2b — OR collapse 2a and 2b into a single delivery so the leaf satisfies parent AC2 directly.
evidence: Parent 716f Decomposition: 'Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism … and add the limit-lock gate.'
Parent 716f AC2: 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files + the sync contract test + the shell gate's allowlist branch) is removed'
Leaf What item 5: 'KEEP the (now-empty) allowlist + ceilings files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them)'
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files' — Phase 2b is not described in the parent at all
findings.finding: The leaf contradicts parent AC2 ('The allowlist/ceilings mechanism … is removed') by explicitly keeping the allowlist and ceilings files (What item 5, Notes section), and it contradicts the parent's Phase 2 decomposition ('one child story') by splitting delivery into an unsanctioned 2a + 2b without updating the parent.
findings.location: Leaf: What item 5 / Notes section; Parent 716f: Phase 2 Decomposition, AC2
scenarios: Phase 2a lands on main; Phase 2b is never written or lands late — parent AC2 remains permanently unsatisfied with the mechanism files still present.
A reviewer checking parent AC2 after 2a merges sees the allowlist/ceilings files still exist and correctly flags non-compliance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 2b ticket and update parent epic 716f's Decomposition section to read: 'Phase 2a — rewrite gate + tests, add limit-lock (files kept for CI ordering); Phase 2b — delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune docs/architecture.md, update AGENTS.md.' Adjust parent AC2 to be satisfied only after both 2a and 2b land, or mark AC2 as a Phase 2b deliverable.

findings.finding: The leaf splits the parent's 'Phase 2 — one child story' into Phase 2a + Phase 2b without updating the parent, and explicitly keeps files the parent AC2 says must be removed (allowlist/ceilings), contradicting the parent's stated decomposition and acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Leaf What §5 / Notes; Parent Decomposition 'Phase 2' and Parent AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In the parent epic (716f), revise the Decomposition to read: 'Phase 2a — swap the gate to absolute cap + limit-lock (keeps legacy files for CI ordering); Phase 2b — delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune architecture.md offender table, update AGENTS.md.' Update AC2 to reference Phase 2b as the completion point. Then re-review the parent, after which this leaf is a clean subset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either include the docs/architecture.md and AGENTS.md updates in Phase 2a (so parent AC3 'documented' is fully satisfied on this change), or update the parent epic to explicitly assign 'documented' to Phase 2b and create a Phase 2b ticket.
evidence: Parent 716f AC3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
Parent 716f file_impact: {"path": "docs/architecture.md", "reason": "offenders table pruned"}
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md'
findings.finding: The leaf defers the docs/architecture.md offenders-table pruning and AGENTS.md update to Phase 2b, contradicting parent AC3 ('documented') and the parent's file_impact entry for docs/architecture.md — so after Phase 2a lands, the 'documented' requirement of parent AC3 is unsatisfied.
findings.location: Leaf: Notes section ('prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'); Parent 716f: AC3, file_impact docs/architecture.md
scenarios: Phase 2a merges; contributors consult docs/architecture.md or AGENTS.md and find stale information about the allowlist escape-hatch that no longer applies to the new absolute-cap gate.
Parent epic 716f is reviewed for closure after 2a lands; AC3's 'documented' sub-requirement is unmet because the docs update was deferred.

findings.finding: What item 5 describes a preservation deliverable — 'KEEP the (now-empty) `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` files in this change' — but no AC verifies that these files still exist after the change lands. This matters because their presence is load-bearing for Phase 2a's CI green-pass on main.
findings.location: What item 5 / Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `- [ ] Legacy allowlist/ceilings files are preserved (verify: \`test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt\`)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the gate script how `origin/main` is made available (e.g., `git fetch origin main` before the comparison) and define explicit error handling if the fetch or `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` fails (e.g., treat a missing ref as 'no base limit' = bootstrap mode, log a warning, continue).
evidence: 'compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail with an ::error:: saying an administrator must override the gate'
'Bootstrap: on THIS change origin/main has no limit file, so the lock does not fire'
ABSENCE: No mention of `git fetch origin main` or ensuring the ref is present before comparison, nor error handling if `git show origin/main:...` exits non-zero due to missing ref or shallow clone.
findings.finding: The limit-lock comparison (`compare against origin/main's limit`) depends on `origin/main` being fetchable and the file being accessible via git, but the plan does not specify how to ensure `origin/main` is available in CI (shallow clones are common and may not have this ref), nor what error/fallback behavior applies if the `origin/main` ref is missing or the fetch fails.
findings.location: What section, item 2c: 'LIMIT LOCK: compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail'
scenarios: CI runner uses a shallow clone (`--depth=1`) and `origin/main` ref is not fetched — the git command to read origin/main's limit file silently fails or errors, causing either a false-positive gate failure or a silently skipped lock check.
Network issue prevents fetching origin/main during CI — behavior is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step before the lock check: `git fetch origin main --depth=1 2>/dev/null || true`, then use `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')` and treat an empty BASE as bootstrap mode (skip lock check), matching the stated bootstrap intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicit error handling for the `git fetch` call in the limit-lock: on fetch failure, either emit a `::error::` with a 'recheck' instruction and `exit 1` (fail-closed, matching the require-ticket pattern), or document that fetch failure is treated as 'no base limit' (bootstrap mode) with a warning — and choose one consistently for both workflows.
evidence: Plan: 'LIMIT LOCK: compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail with an ::error::'
Plan: 'Bootstrap: on THIS change origin/main has no limit file, so the lock does not fire' — implies empty/missing BASE is the 'not fire' signal
ABSENCE: no specified error handling for the `git fetch` call itself (distinct from `git show`)

Existing pattern in gerrit-verify.yaml require-ticket: uses a retry loop with `for attempt in 1 2 3` and explicit `exit 1` on failure with an informative error message — the plan's limit-lock fetch has no analogous resilience specification
test.yml uses `actions/checkout@v7` with default fetch-depth=1 (shallow); `origin/main` may not be a locally-known ref without an explicit fetch
findings.finding: The limit-lock step has an unhandled failure mode: if the `git fetch` for `origin/main` fails (transient network error, authentication, or shallow-clone environment), the plan's implied `|| echo ''` guard applies only to `git show`, not to `git fetch`. Under `set -euo pipefail` a non-zero fetch exit hard-fails the gate step — blocking unrelated legitimate changes on a transient infrastructure issue with no actionable error message. Conversely, if the plan intends to suppress the fetch failure (e.g., `|| true`), a failed fetch means `BASE=''` and the lock silently never fires. Neither outcome is specified or mitigated.
findings.location: What § 2c — LIMIT LOCK; Notes 'E5: the gate is shell'
scenarios: CI network transient causes `git fetch` to exit non-zero; `set -euo pipefail` aborts the Module-size gate step; every change on that CI run fails with a cryptic shell error rather than a module-size message.
Fetch succeeds but returns empty (race/timing); BASE=''; limit change slips the lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make ACs 4 and 5 self-contained by providing the concrete shell invocation (e.g. copy the gate's shell body into a temp script, set env vars, and run `bash /tmp/gate_test.sh`), or state that `bash -c '...'` inline tests are acceptable and provide the literal snippet.
evidence: AC4: 'extract the gate step, remove .github/module-size-limit.txt, run it → exit 1'
AC5: 'the lock snippet with BASE=800 LIMIT=900 → exit 1 with the override message; BASE=800 LIMIT=800 → exit 0'
Neither AC explains whether extraction means copy-paste to a temp shell file, use `act`, run a specific helper, or something else — leaving a real design choice to the executor.
findings.finding: ACs 4 and 5 instruct the executor to 'extract the gate step' and test 'the lock snippet' without specifying how to extract/invoke the shell fragment — the proving command is underspecified and requires executor judgment to construct.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('extract the gate step, remove .github/module-size-limit.txt, run it') and AC5 ('the lock snippet with BASE=800 LIMIT=900')
scenarios: Executor interprets 'extract' differently from the author; the test they write exercises a subtly different code path, giving a false green on AC4/5.
findings.suggested_fix:
For AC4:
```bash
# Write the gate body to a temp file, remove the limit file, run:
bash -c 'LIMIT=$(cat .github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null) || { echo "::error:: module-size-limit.txt must not be removed"; exit 1; }'
# Verify: exit code 1 and 'must not be removed' in output
```
For AC5, add explicit env-var invocation:
```bash
BASE=800 LIMIT=900 bash -c '<lock-check snippet>' # expect exit 1
BASE=800 LIMIT=800 bash -c '<lock-check snippet>' # expect exit 0
```
Pin '<lock-check snippet>' to the exact lines that will appear in the workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the parenthetical in What item 2: the parity test uses COMMAND SIGNATURES (not step names); clarify that the step name can stay unchanged for readability, but item 4's `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` update to `'module-size-limit.txt'` is the actual required mechanism for parity-test correctness.
evidence: tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py line 46: 'Each gating check keyed by a STABLE command signature (not the step name — names differ in wording between the two files)'
tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py line 50: '"module-size ratchet": "module-size-ceilings.txt"' — the test looks for the string 'module-size-ceilings.txt' in both workflow files, NOT a step name

Plan's What item 4 correctly says to change the signature to 'module-size-limit.txt', but the parenthetical rationale in item 2 states the step NAME is what the parity test uses — contradicted by the code
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'keep the step NAME "Module-size gate" so the parity test still finds it' — but the actual parity test (test_ci_workflow_parity.py) explicitly does NOT use step names; it uses command-string signatures in _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES (line 46: 'not the step name — names differ in wording between the two files'). The current module-size entry is keyed on the signature 'module-size-ceilings.txt', which will vanish from both workflows after this change. The plan's item 4 correctly updates the signature to 'module-size-limit.txt', which IS the load-bearing mechanism — but calling the step name the reason could mislead an implementer into treating item 4's signature update as optional cosmetics, causing the parity test to fail.
findings.location: ## What, item 2 — '(keep the step NAME "Module-size gate" so the parity test still finds it)'
scenarios: An implementer reads the parenthetical, concludes the step name is what matters, and treats item 4's signature update as just a label change. If they do not update _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES, test_ci_workflow_parity.py will fail because 'module-size-ceilings.txt' no longer appears in either workflow.
An implementer renames the step away from 'Module-size gate' and, misled by the rationale, thinks that breaks the parity test — when it does not.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(keep the step NAME "Module-size gate" so the parity test still finds it)' to '(the step name may stay "Module-size gate" for readability; the parity test uses command signatures, not step names — item 4's _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES update to "module-size-limit.txt" is what keeps test_ci_workflow_parity.py passing)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the limit-lock fetch uses the explicit GitHub URL (matching the pattern all other cross-repo fetches in `gerrit-verify.yaml` use: `git fetch "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main`) rather than `git fetch origin main`, to guarantee it reaches GitHub's `main` in both the Gerrit and GitHub Actions checkout contexts.
evidence: Plan: 'LIMIT LOCK: compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail'
gerrit-verify.yaml require-ticket step: `git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" '+tickets:refs/remotes/gh-tickets'` — uses explicit GitHub URL, not `origin`
gerrit-verify.yaml verify-identity step: `git fetch "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" ...` — same pattern

ADR-0021: checkout-gerrit-change-action fetches GERRIT_REFSPEC from the GitHub mirror (or direct Gerrit fallback); the `origin` remote points to the Gerrit-side/mirror remote, not necessarily github.com's main branch
ABSENCE: the plan provides no explicit `git fetch` URL for the limit-lock, unlike all other GitHub-data fetches in the same workflow
findings.finding: The limit-lock mechanism as described ('compare against origin/main's limit') assumes `git fetch origin main` + `git show origin/main:...` will reliably reach GitHub's main branch from within `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s build-and-test job. However, every existing cross-repo git operation in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (require-ticket, verify-identity) explicitly uses `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` rather than `origin`, because `checkout-gerrit-change-action` configures `origin` to the Gerrit/mirror remote — not GitHub's `main`. If `git fetch -q origin main` fetches Gerrit refs (or a mirror without a `main` branch), `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` returns nothing, `BASE=''`, and the lock condition `[ -n "$BASE" ] && [ "$BASE" != "$LIMIT" ]` never fires — the admin lock is silently inoperative in the pre-merge Verified gate.
findings.location: What § 2c — LIMIT LOCK; gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job
scenarios: In the gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job, `git fetch -q origin main` fetches Gerrit mirror refs, not GitHub's main; `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` fails silently; BASE=''; lock never fires; a contributor changes module-size-limit.txt and the limit-change lands without admin force-submit.

Even in test.yml (where `actions/checkout@v7` configures origin to GitHub), a shallow clone (default fetch-depth=1) means `origin/main` is not a fetchable ref until explicitly fetched — same silent-bypass risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git fetch -q origin main` with `git fetch -q "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main` (or `refs/heads/main:refs/remotes/origin/main`) so the fetch is context-independent, mirroring the pattern used by require-ticket and verify-identity in the same workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify in the plan how `origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` is fetched in the `gerrit-verify.yaml` context (e.g., by reusing the `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` fetch pattern already used in require-ticket/verify-identity), and document the bootstrap behavior when the fetch yields no file.
evidence: gerrit-verify.yaml step: `uses: lfreleng-actions/checkout-gerrit-change-action@f87b90a3370e62dad310797fedc7fd3700c75832` — a custom Gerrit checkout action, not standard `actions/checkout`.
Plan states limit-lock: 'compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail' — this implies a `git fetch origin main` + `git show origin/main:...` in the runner.

Existing workflow already has a pattern for fetching from GitHub (`git fetch ... 'https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}'`) in the require-ticket and verify-identity steps, but the plan does not specify that the limit-lock will use this same pattern vs. assuming a pre-configured `origin` remote.
Bootstrap caveat 'on THIS change origin/main has no limit file, so the lock does not fire' suggests the plan intends a git-fetch approach, but the exact mechanism is unspecified.
findings.finding: The limit-lock step (What §2c) relies on `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` being resolvable in the CI runner environment, but the `gerrit-verify.yaml` workflow uses `checkout-gerrit-change-action` (a custom action that may produce a shallow/detached checkout without a configured `origin/main` remote) rather than `actions/checkout`. Whether `origin` is set and `main` is fetchable in that context is an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: What §2c (LIMIT LOCK) / gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job (uses checkout-gerrit-change-action)
scenarios: If `checkout-gerrit-change-action` does not configure `origin` to point to the GitHub repo (or produces a shallow clone that lacks the `main` branch), `git show origin/main:...` silently returns empty or errors, causing the lock to either silently pass or noisily fail on every change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `origin` resolves to GitHub with `main` reachable in `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `checkout-gerrit-change-action` context, or replace `git fetch -q origin main` with the explicit URL pattern already established in that workflow (`git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main`).
evidence: Plan text (item 2c): 'compare against origin/main's limit; if it exists and differs, fail'
gerrit-verify.yaml require-ticket step uses explicit URL: 'git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" "+tickets:refs/remotes/gh-tickets"' — avoids `origin`
gerrit-verify.yaml verify-identity step: 'git fetch "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" ...' — same explicit-URL pattern
ADR 0021: checkout-gerrit-change-action is 'mirror-first' (GitHub mirror → Gerrit fallback), but does not guarantee `origin` = GitHub with `main` available
Evidence class: Partially-verified (git fetch/show exist and work in test.yml's actions/checkout@v7 context; UNVERIFIED for the checkout-gerrit-change-action context in gerrit-verify.yaml)
findings.finding: The limit-lock mechanism (`git fetch -q origin main` then `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt`) depends on `origin` resolving to the GitHub remote with a reachable `main` branch. In `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `build-and-test` job, `checkout-gerrit-change-action` checks out the patchset; every other step in that workflow that needs GitHub-branch data explicitly uses `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` (not `origin`) — a strong signal that `origin` in that runner context may not reliably resolve to GitHub/main. If `git fetch -q origin main` silently exits 0 but fetches a Gerrit ref (or fails), `git show origin/main:...` returns empty, `$BASE` stays empty, and the lock never fires in the Gerrit-verified lane — making the admin-lock inoperative precisely where it matters most.
findings.location: What / item 2c — limit-lock shell snippet; gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job context
scenarios: In the gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job, `git fetch -q origin main` silently fails or fetches a Gerrit-server remote; `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` returns empty; `$BASE` is empty string; the `if [ -n "$BASE" ] && [ "$BASE" != "$LIMIT" ]` guard short-circuits; the admin lock never fires for any limit change submitted via Gerrit — defeating its entire purpose.
An operator changes 800→900 in the limit file and submits via Gerrit; the lock is inoperative in the Verified lane so the change gets +1 even without admin force-submit.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the limit-lock fetch with the explicit URL used by every other GitHub-branch fetch in gerrit-verify.yaml:
```bash
git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main 2>/dev/null || true
BASE=$(git show FETCH_HEAD:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')
```
This is the pattern already established by `require-ticket` and `verify-identity` and is verified to work in the checkout-gerrit-change-action context.

findings.finding: The limit-lock snippet (`git fetch -q origin main` then `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt`) assumes `origin` points to the GitHub repo where `main` lives, but in `gerrit-verify.yaml` the `checkout-gerrit-change-action` may configure `origin` to the Gerrit server/mirror rather than the GitHub remote. The existing `require-ticket` and `verify-identity` steps in `gerrit-verify.yaml` explicitly fetch via `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` instead of `origin main`, which is a strong signal that `origin` in that context does not reliably resolve to GitHub's `main`. If `git fetch -q origin main` fails silently (exit 0) or fetches a Gerrit ref without the limit file, `$BASE` becomes empty and the lock never fires — meaning the lock is inoperative in the Gerrit-verified lane.
findings.location: What / item 2c — limit-lock shell snippet; gerrit-verify.yaml context
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git fetch -q origin main` with `git fetch -q "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main` and adjust `git show` to `git show FETCH_HEAD:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (or keep the `origin/main` ref after the fetch sets it). This mirrors the proven pattern from `require-ticket`/`verify-identity` in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and is safe in `test.yml` too since `${{ github.repository }}` is always available.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
verify command `wc -l` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -q 'must override' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && grep -q 'must override' .github/workflows/test.yml` greps the unanchored word 'must' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_must'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"must":')
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR (or an AGENTS.md stub) in Phase 2a itself documenting the admin-lock governance decision and the force-submit escalation path, so contributors aren't blocked without explanation between Phase 2a and Phase 2b.
evidence: 'Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL).'
'Phase 2b... updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'
No ADR or architecture decision record is mentioned anywhere in the plan, even for Phase 2b.
findings.finding: The admin-locked limit mechanism (force-submit-only gate for cap changes) is a novel architectural/governance decision explicitly called out as an 'operator decision', but no ADR and no immediate AGENTS.md update is planned for Phase 2a. Phase 2b mentions updating AGENTS.md but still no ADR. Between Phase 2a landing and Phase 2b landing, contributors encounter a new gate behavior (limit-lock requiring admin override) with no documented rationale or process.
findings.location: Notes section / Phase 2b description
scenarios: A contributor tries to change the 800 cap via normal PR, hits 'administrator must override' with no docs explaining why or how to escalate.
A future reviewer asks why the CI-gate-in-code approach was chosen over Gerrit ACL — no ADR to consult.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNNN-module-size-admin-lock.md` (or an AGENTS.md section) in this PR explaining: (1) why CI-gate-in-code was chosen over Gerrit ACL, (2) what 'administrator force-submit' means operationally, and (3) when/how a limit change is legitimate. Phase 2b can then expand it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add the `docs/architecture.md` gate-mechanism description paragraph (lines 253–261, which names `module-size-allowlist.txt` as the gate's source of truth) to Phase 2b's edit scope, or update it in Phase 2a alongside the gate rewrite.
evidence: Phase 2b note: 'prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table' — refers to the table at line 263, not the gate description paragraph at lines 253–261
docs/architecture.md line 255: 'LOC and is not in `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` — so a *new* offender cannot land silently.'
docs/architecture.md line 256: '`.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` is the source of truth for the over-cap set'
After 2a the gate reads `module-size-limit.txt` with no allowlist, making this paragraph factually wrong for any agent or contributor who reads it
findings.finding: The `docs/architecture.md` gate-mechanism description paragraph (lines 253–261) names `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` as 'the source of truth for the over-cap set' and describes the old allowlist-based gate. After Phase 2a lands, this paragraph actively misdescribes the live gate to agents and contributors. Phase 2b's stated scope is 'prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table' (the table starting at line 263) — a distinct section from the mechanism description paragraph. This consumer is unenumerated: Phase 2b as written does not clearly cover updating the gate-mechanism paragraph.
findings.location: Notes / Phase 2b description; docs/architecture.md lines 253–261
scenarios: An agent editing a large file post-2a reads AGENTS.md (already deferred) and architecture.md and follows the allowlist instructions — but the gate now ignores the allowlist entirely, so the agent's change appears correct locally but fails CI for different reasons.
A contributor tries to add a file to `module-size-allowlist.txt` following the architecture.md instructions and finds the gate doesn't honor it.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand Phase 2b's Notes entry to say: 'prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table AND updates the gate-mechanism description paragraph (lines 253–261) to reference module-size-limit.txt as the new single source of truth.' Alternatively, update those lines in 2a since they describe the gate mechanism, not the per-file offenders data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the 'Why' section explaining why native platform enforcement (Gerrit submit requirements / CODEOWNERS + required review on `.github/module-size-limit.txt`) is insufficient for this deployment, so the in-CI limit-lock approach is the justified choice.
evidence: Plan states: 'Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL)' — the dismissal is a parenthetical with no rationale.

Established alternatives exist: Gerrit submit requirements that require a privileged label for the limit file change; GitHub CODEOWNERS with required review on `.github/module-size-limit.txt`; Gerrit Label configuration with exclusive permissions on that file path.
ABSENCE: No articulation of why these alternatives are ruled out (e.g., Gerrit submit requirements not configured in this deployment, CODEOWNERS not enforced on the GitHub mirror, etc.).
The limit-lock pattern does not appear anywhere in the existing workflow files (gerrit-verify.yaml, test.yml), confirming it is novel to this codebase.
findings.finding: The 'limit-lock' mechanism — comparing `origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` against the working-tree value in-CI to enforce admin-only overrides — is a novel pattern for this codebase. The plan dismisses established alternatives (Gerrit submit requirements, CODEOWNERS + required reviews on the limit file) with only 'no Gerrit ACL' but provides no rationale for why those alternatives are inadequate, so reviewers cannot assess whether the bespoke approach is the right trade-off.
findings.location: Why section: 'Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL).' / What item 2c (LIMIT LOCK)
scenarios: A reviewer cannot assess whether the bespoke in-CI veto is the right trade-off vs. a Gerrit label approach that would be simpler and not subject to git-fetch availability issues.
If the operator later configures Gerrit submit requirements, the in-CI veto becomes redundant dead code with no documented rationale for removal.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why' section, after '(no Gerrit ACL)', add 1–2 sentences such as: 'The Gerrit deployment is operator-managed and cannot be assumed to have submit-requirement configuration available; CODEOWNERS enforcement requires GitHub branch-protection rules that are not guaranteed on the mirror repo. The in-CI gate provides a self-contained guarantee that is version-controlled alongside the code it protects.'

findings.finding: Step 2c specifies a 'fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change' behavior (guarding deletion of the lock), but no acceptance criterion verifies this missing-file path fires correctly.
findings.location: What / step 2c ('Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock).')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Simulate missing limit file: unset LIMIT (or set it to empty); run the gate snippet; assert exit 1 with an appropriate error message.'

findings.finding: The 'missing limit file guard' behavior (gate fails if .github/module-size-limit.txt is absent from the change) is introduced in What §2c but has no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What — §2c ('Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock)') / Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: AC2's allowlist-absence check (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist'`) only verifies `gerrit-verify.yaml`; `test.yml` is not checked, so the new gate in `test.yml` could still read the old allowlist and the AC would silently pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second AC bullet (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single-file grep with: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.finding: AC bullet 2's verify step checks only `gerrit-verify.yaml` for absence of allowlist reads (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`), but What item 2d says BOTH workflows 'no longer read' those files. The equivalent check against `test.yml` is absent, leaving described removal work in test.yml uncovered by any criterion.
findings.location: AC bullet 2 / What item 2d
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 2: `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml` to the verify command.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
